title: Tapa — Pacific bark cloth as paper
materials: paper mulberry (kozo) bark strips, soaked overnight
smooth rounded stone or glass bottle for beating
flat wooden board as beating surface
sources: Tapa-making tradition documented in Augustin Kramer, The Samoa Islands (1902) — public domain. Also referenced in Henry Ling Roth, Studies in Primitive Looms (1918) — public domain. Pacific Island tapa-making tradition predates writing paper.
notes: Tapa (Pacific), amate (Mesoamerica), and Egyptian papyrus are the same basic idea: beating plant-bark fibres until they mat together without water-formation on a mould. This tutorial covers tapa-making with kozo bark (also the material for Japanese washi, so supply chains overlap). Covers: soaking the bark strips overnight, beating with a smooth stone on a flat board (the fibres mat together by felting), layering two beaten strips at right angles for strength, drying flat under weight. The resulting sheet is stiffer and more textile-like than mould-formed paper. Natural surface variation is part of the aesthetic. Distinguish from mould-and-deckle sheet forming (no water suspension needed here).
